items.title: Digest #181: GitHub Actions Bypass, K8s Databases, Azure Token Flaw, Postgres Partitioning and Running Linux in Docker
items.author: Mohamed Labouardy
items.content:
Welcome to this week’s edition of the DevOps Bulletin!

GitHub Actions just got called out for a fork-based bypass, letting imposter commits sneak past reviews. Kubernetes, long mocked for struggling with stateful workloads, now has operators that make managing Postgres clusters as easy as deployments. Grab explained how it built an auth system for 180M users, while a nasty bug in Azure Entra ID’s actor tokens let attackers jump tenants and grab Global Admin. Oh, and Tinder shared how its API gateway handles a billion swipes a day.

On the hands-on side: Postgres partitioning best practices, Docker networking made simple, bots writing bad Terraform, attacker persistence in Kubernetes, and a new EnvFiles trick in Kubernetes 1.34. Plus: Vault with OpenBao, caching in Postgres vs Redis, doing infra code reviews like a pro, and even running a full Linux desktop in Docker.

And don’t miss the projects: Kingfisher (secret scanning), GitButler (branch management reimagined), LingoDB (linguistics as 3D data), HexStrike AI (150+ cyber tools for AI agents), Arcane (Docker UI), Alchemist (macOS command helper), and WinApps (Windows apps on Linux).

All this and more in this week’s DevOps Bulletin, don’t miss out!

DevOps, stop wasting time on job boards. Jack & Jill are AI Super-Recruiters: Jack finds engineers real jobs, Jill sends companies great-fit DevOps hires. Faster, smarter, cheaper than recruiters — no ghosting, no chaos.

Consider supporting it with a paid subscription. You’ll keep the free Friday issues get extras like bonus deep-dives, templates, and the full archive.

📘 New Book:

This book is written from years of running FinOps at scale: what worked (and what didn’t) is now in this .

  • is a blazingly fast tool for secret detection and live validation across files, Git repos, S3, Docker images, Jira, Slack, and Confluence

  • is a high-performance linguistic database that represents language as a 3D spatial structure.

  • is a git branch management tool, built from the ground up for modern workflows.

  • is an advanced MCP server that lets AI agents (Claude, GPT, Copilot, etc.) autonomously run 150+ cybersecurity tools for automated pentesting

  •  is a modern, easy-to-use way to manage your Docker containers, images, volumes, and networks, all in one place.

  • is a smart command-not-found handler for macOS that automatically suggests Homebrew packages when you type an unrecognized command.

  • apps such as Microsoft Office/Adobe in Linux (Ubuntu/Fedora) and GNOME/KDE.

If you have feedback to share or are interested in sponsoring this newsletter, feel free to reach out via , or simply reply to this email.

items.title: The Cloud Native Sign Language Glossary: A Story of Community and Inclusion
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>What is Kubernetes in sign language? How do you sign “CNCF”? For the first time, the cloud native community has signs for these terms.

Join us for a special panel to celebrate the collaborative journey of creating the Cloud Native Sign Language Glossary, a community effort to make the tech ecosystem more accessible for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing community.

Discover the inspiration behind the project, the challenges of creating signs for complex concepts like 'Kubernetes' and 'service mesh', and the impact this will have on professional and educational settings. Don't miss this inspiring conversation with the glossary's creators as we discuss what's next and how you can get involved.</article>

items.title: Ansible for Network & Server Automation: A Practical, In-Depth Guide
items.author: Scaibu
items.content:
Learn how Ansible’s architecture differs for Linux vs. network devices, and build production-ready inventories, variables, and playbooks — plus idempotent deployments, reporting, compliance, and scaling patterns.

  • What Is Ansible? (Architecture You Actually Use)
  • Linux Servers vs. Network Devices (Execution Model — No Tables)
  • Core Vocabulary (Inventory, Playbooks, Plays, Tasks, Modules)
  • Build an Inventory (Groups, Children, Variables, OS Tags)
  • Variables & Precedence (Group vs. Host vs. all)
  • Write & Run Your First Playbook (and Read the Output)
  • Secrets with Ansible Vault (No Plain-Text Passwords)
  • Move Variables into Files (group_vars & host_vars)
  • Network Automation Playbooks (Templates, Idempotency, Conditions)
  • Gather Facts, Debug Output, and Save Command Results
  • Compliance Checks (with set_fact and assert)
  • Generate Reports (Markdown without Tables)
  • Use Third-Party Collections (NAPALM & NTC)
  • Production Best Practices & Troubleshooting

What Is Ansible? (Architecture You Actually Use)

Ansible is an automation tool. One (where Ansible is installed) connects outward and performs work.

  • For , the control host SSHs into each server, copies tiny Python modules, and runs them on that machine.
  • For , Ansible typically runs in on the control host. It into devices; instead, it talks via SSH, NETCONF, HTTP APIs, or SNMP and pushes commands or RPCs from the control node.

Some Linux-based NOSes can run Python internally, but the default mental model for networks is .

Linux Servers vs. Network Devices: Understanding the Execution Model

When working with Ansible, the way tasks are executed depends heavily on whether you are automating or . At first glance, both may look similar because they share transports like SSH, but under the hood the execution model differs significantly. Let’s break it down step by step.

  • rely primarily on SSH for communication. The control node establishes an SSH session with each server and pushes automation instructions.
  • , while they can use SSH, often provide additional options such as NETCONF (used by platforms like Junos), HTTP APIs (such as Arista EOS eAPI or Cisco NX-API), or even SNMP for read-only access. This flexibility is necessary because not all network operating systems support running Python code natively.
  • On , Ansible modules (written in Python) are physically copied to the target machine and executed there. In other words, the automation logic runs directly on the host being managed.
  • On , the execution happens on the . Instead of pushing Python to the device, Ansible simply sends CLI commands, API calls, or RPCs across the session. The device processes those commands, but the actual code never leaves the controller.

3. Parallelism in Execution

  • In a Linux automation workflow, each server is responsible for running its own module locally. This distributed approach means 100 servers each execute their own piece of code.
  • In , the control node acts as the central executor. It fans out to multiple devices at once, sending the required instructions concurrently, while still keeping the intelligence on the controller side.

4. Artefacts on the Device

  • For , every task involves uploading small Python snippets or modules that perform the job, whether it’s restarting a process, installing a package, or editing a configuration file.
  • For , no such files are ever copied. The control node issues commands or structured RPC calls. The device interprets them and applies changes without ever storing or executing external Python code.

Core Vocabulary in Ansible: Inventory, Playbooks, Plays, Tasks, and Modules

To work effectively with Ansible, it’s important to get comfortable with its . These terms form the foundation of any automation workflow and describe how Ansible organizes infrastructure, defines instructions, and executes actions. Let’s walk through each one.

The is the starting point of any Ansible project. It’s essentially a list of hosts — servers, switches, routers, or any device — that you want to automate.

  • Hosts can be organized into (for example, all data center switches or all Linux web servers).
  • , allowing you to build hierarchical structures that mirror your real-world environment (e.g., region → site → device role).
  • Inventories can also carry , meaning you can attach parameters like usernames, passwords, or OS types directly to groups or hosts for dynamic automation.

In practice, the inventory becomes your map of the infrastructure that Ansible will control.

A is where you describe automation in detail. Written in YAML, it defines:

  • , which set the scope (what hosts or groups to target) and the strategy for connecting to them.
  • , which outline the actions you want to perform.

Think of a playbook as the script of your automation story — it decides who’s involved and what will happen step by step.

A is one section within a playbook. It answers two big questions:

  1. Which hosts am I targeting?
  2. How should tasks be executed against them?

Each play is like a chapter in your automation workflow, focusing on a specific part of the infrastructure. For example, one play might configure routers, while another handles Linux servers.

Inside each play, you define . A task represents a single unit of work — installing a package, pushing a config line, restarting a service, or gathering data. Tasks are executed in order, top to bottom, and they’re what make the playbook “do things.”

Tasks rely on modules to carry out the actual work, but they give structure and readability to your automation logic.

Modules are the . They are small, purpose-built programs that handle specific tasks. Examples include:

  • ios_command for issuing commands to Cisco IOS devices
  • nxos_config for applying the configuration on NX-OS
  • junos_config for Juniper devices

Modules make Ansible powerful because they abstract away the complexity. Instead of writing raw CLI scripts, you use modules that understand how to safely interact with the device or server.

The most critical concept in Ansible vocabulary is . It means that when you run a playbook multiple times, it won’t keep reapplying the same changes unnecessarily.

  • If the desired state already exists, Ansible will leave it as is.
  • If something is missing, only then will it make the change.

This ensures automation is safe, repeatable, and predictable — a huge difference from manual scripts that may blindly push configurations every time.

Building an Ansible Inventory: Groups, Children, Variables, and OS Tags

One of the first steps in mastering Ansible is learning how to build an . The inventory is where you define the devices and servers that Ansible will manage, along with their relationships, variables, and attributes. At first it may look simple — just a list of IPs — but as your environment grows, structuring it properly becomes essential.

Let’s walk through how to design an inventory that’s both flexible and production-ready.

The simplest form of inventory is just a flat list of hosts. You can write this in either or .

10.1.100.10  10.5.10.10  
all:  hosts:    "10.5.10.10":

Both formats describe the same thing: three devices, defined by either IP addresses or hostnames. YAML is more explicit and structured, while INI is lightweight and quick to write.

2. Organising by Regions and Roles

As soon as you scale beyond a few devices, it becomes necessary to group them. Groups let you organise hosts by function, geography, or any logical pattern.

For example, you might have:

  • Customer-premises devices (CPEs) like routers at branch offices
  • like spine switches
  • Different such as AMERS and EMEA

Here’s how that looks in practice (using INI notation):

[amers-cpe]  csr1  nxos-spine1  amers-cpe  vmx1  eos-spine1  emea-cpe  amers-cpe  amers-dc
  • : e.g., amers-cpe or emea-dc.
  • : e.g., amers and emea, which are of device groups.
  • : e.g., all-cpe and all-dc, which cut across regions.

This structure allows you to automate at any level: a single device, a role, a region, or the entire infrastructure.

Another best practice is tagging devices by their network operating system (NOS). Many Ansible modules are OS-specific, so tagging ensures the right templates and modules are applied.

[nxos]  nxos-spine1  [nxos:vars]  eos-spine1  [eos:vars]  csr1  [iosxe:vars]  vmx1  [junos:vars]

With OS tagging, a single playbook can apply configurations across multiple vendors, while still running the correct module per platform.

4. Using Variables in Groups

Groups aren’t just about organisation — they’re also a natural place to define . Common examples include:

  • ansible_user (username for login)
  • ansible_password (authentication)
  • ansible_port (SSH/NETCONF/HTTP port)
  • Privilege escalation settings like ansible_become or ansible_become_method=enable

Defining these at the group level ensures consistency across devices, while still allowing you to override them for specific hosts when necessary.

Why Inventory Design Matters

A well-structured inventory is more than just bookkeeping — it’s the foundation of scalable, reusable automation. By grouping devices logically, tagging them by OS, and attaching the right variables, you make it easier to write playbooks that work across diverse environments.

Start small with flat lists, then evolve toward groups, children, and variables as your projects grow. A clean inventory makes every automation effort smoother, safer, and easier to maintain.

Variables and Precedence in Ansible

Variables are what make Ansible powerful and flexible. They let you parameterize your automation so that playbooks can adapt to different regions, roles, operating systems, or even individual devices. But when multiple variables overlap, you need to know which one wins. That’s where come into play.

1. The Precedence Hierarchy

The general rule of thumb is:

  1. Defaults at the global level ( – These apply everywhere as baseline values.
  2. Region or role group variables — Narrow the scope by overriding global defaults.
  3.  — Fine-tune based on device type.
  4.  — The final authority; they override everything else.

This layered system gives you the flexibility to define values once and only override them where absolutely necessary.

2. Example: NTP and Syslog

Here’s a simple inventory snippet that shows how precedence works:

[amers:vars]  ntp_server=10.1.200.11  ntp_server=10.10.200.11  nxos-spine1 ntp_server=10.1.200.200 syslog_server=10.1.200.201  syslog_server=10.1.200.201
  • At the , every device inherits the same syslog server.
  • At the , devices in each geography get their own NTP server.
  • At the , nxos-spine1 overrides even the regional setting and uses a custom NTP and syslog configuration.
The lesson: always start broad, and only override when the context demands it. This keeps your inventory clean and predictable.

Writing and Running Your First Playbook

Once your inventory and variables are in place, the next step is to write a playbook. A playbook describes you want to do, to do it, and it should be executed.

Here’s a simple example that audits and then configures SNMP on Cisco IOS XE devices:

# snmp-intro.yml---
- name: PLAY 1 - ISSUE SNMP COMMANDS
  hosts: iosxe  connection: ansible.netcommon.network_cli    - name: TASK 1 - SHOW SNMP CONFIG        commands:
    - name: TASK 2 - DEPLOY SNMP COMMANDS
      cisco.ios.ios_config:          - "snmp-server chassis-id {{ inventory_hostname }}"
ansible-playbook snmp-intro.yml -i inventory \  -e ansible_user="ntc" -e ansible_password="ntc123"
  • --check: Run in dry-run mode to preview changes without applying them.
  • --diff: Show the difference between the current and intended configuration.
  • -v to -vvvv: Increase verbosity for debugging (up to connection-level detail).
  • --limit csr1,csr2: Restrict execution to a subset of hosts for safe testing.

These flags give you control over how playbooks run and help avoid surprises in production.

Secrets with Ansible Vault (No Plain-Text Passwords)

Storing usernames and passwords in plain text is a security risk. Ansible Vault solves this problem by letting you encrypt sensitive data.

  1. Create and Encrypt a Secrets File
cat > secrets.enc << 'EOF'ansible_user: ntcEOF
ansible-vault encrypt secrets.enc

2. Use the Vault in a Playbook Run

ansible-playbook snmp-intro.yml -e @secrets.enc --ask-vault-pass

Or, if you’re managing multiple vaults (e.g., dev and prod), use vault IDs:

ansible-playbook snmp-intro.yml \  --vault-id dev@prompt \
  • by marking sensitive tasks with
  • so that teams follow a consistent approach.
With variables, playbooks, and Ansible Vault in place, you now have the core building blocks to run safe, repeatable, and secure automation workflows. The next step is to move variables into structured files (group_vars and host_vars) so your inventory stays lean while scaling to hundreds or thousands of devices.

Move Variables into Files (group_vars & host_vars)

Recommended project shape:

project/├─ inventory│  ├─ all.yml│  └─ emea/│     └─ interfaces.yml   ├─ csr1.yml      ├─ ntp.yml

Example group_vars/amers.yml:

---snmp:  location: "AMERICAS-NJ"    - { community: "public",     type: "ro" }
    - { community: "public123",  type: "ro" }
    - { community: "private",    type: "rw" }
    - { community: "secure",     type: "rw" }

Network Automation Playbooks (Templates, Idempotency, Conditions)

Generate per-OS SNMP configs with Jinja

templates/snmp/  eos.j2  junos.j2
snmp-server location {{ snmp.location }}snmp-server contact {{ snmp.contact }}
{% for c in snmp.communities -%}
snmp-server community {{ c.community }} {{ c.type|upper }}
set snmp location {{ snmp.location }}set snmp contact {{ snmp.contact|replace(' ', '_') }}
{% for c in snmp.communities -%}
{% if c.type|lower == "rw" -%}
set snmp community {{ c.community }} authorization read-write
{% elif c.type|lower == "ro" -%}
set snmp community {{ c.community }} authorization read-only
{% endif -%}
---- name: PLAY 1 - GENERATE SNMP CONFIGS  connection: local    - name: Render per-device SNMP config        src: "./templates/snmp/{{ ansible_network_os }}.j2"
        dest: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"

2) Deploy configs idempotently (OS-specific)

- name: PLAY 2 - ENSURE EOS SNMP CONFIG IS DEPLOYED  hosts: eos  gather_facts: no    - arista.eos.eos_config:
        src: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"
- name: PLAY 3 - ENSURE JUNOS SNMP CONFIG IS DEPLOYED  hosts: junos
  connection: ansible.netcommon.netconf
  gather_facts: no    - junipernetworks.junos.junos_config:
        src: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"

Cisco IOS/NX-OS (conditionals):

- name: PLAY 4 - ENSURE CISCO SNMP CONFIG IS DEPLOYED  hosts: "iosxe,nxos"
  connection: ansible.netcommon.network_cli
  gather_facts: no    - name: IOS XE apply        src: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"
      when: ansible_network_os == 'ios'
      cisco.nxos.nxos_config:
        src: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"
      when: ansible_network_os == 'nxos'

config modules fetch the current state, compare with desired lines, and only send missing deltas. Use --check and --diff to preview.

  • Many config modules support backup: true (save pre-change config).
  • NX-OS/IOS XE often support save_when: modified.
  • Junos supports workflows in NETCONF land.

Gather Facts, Debug Output, and Save Command Results

- name: Collect IOS facts  hosts: iosxe
  connection: ansible.netcommon.network_cli
  gather_facts: no    - cisco.ios.ios_facts:
    - debug: { var: ansible_net_version }
    - debug: { var: ansible_net_hostname }

Useful facts (no table — plain list):

  • ansible_net_model — device model.
  • ansible_net_serialnum — serial number.
  • ansible_net_version — OS version string.
  • ansible_net_hostname — configured hostname.
  • ansible_net_config — current running config (if supported).
  • ansible_net_all_ipv4_addresses — all IPv4 addresses.
  • ansible_net_all_ipv6_addresses — all IPv6 addresses.
  • ansible_net_interfaces — dictionary of interfaces.
  • ansible_net_neighbors — LLDP/CDP neighbors (module support varies).

Register output from arbitrary show commands and write to file:

- name: Show SNMP communities  cisco.ios.ios_command:
    commands: [ "show run | inc snmp-server community" ]
  register: snmp_data
- name: Write output to file
  copy:
    content: "{{ snmp_data.stdout[0] }}"
    dest: "./commands/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.txt"

Compliance Checks (with set_fact and assert)

Ensure VLAN 20 exists on EOS:

- name: VLAN compliance on EOS  hosts: eos
  connection: ansible.netcommon.network_cli
  gather_facts: no    - name: Get VLANs (JSON)        commands: [ "show vlan brief | json" ]      set_fact:
        existing_vlan_ids: "{{ vlan_data.stdout[0].vlans.keys() }}"
    - name: Assert VLAN 20 exists
      assert:          - "'20' in existing_vlan_ids"
  • Validate banners, AAA, and SNMP communities.
  • Ensure specific features are disabled (e.g., unused services).
  • Check neighbour topology consistency.

Generate Reports (Markdown without Tables)

Per-device report template (reports/facts.j2) without tables:

# {{ inventory_hostname }}Serial Number: {{ ansible_net_serialnum }}
OS Version: {{ ansible_net_version }}
{% for iface, neighs in ansible_net_neighbors.items() -%}- Interface {{ iface }} -> Neighbor {{ n.host }} (neighbor port {{ n.port }}){% endfor -%}{% for i in ansible_net_interfaces.keys() -%}{% endfor -%}

Playbook to build device reports and a single master report:

---- name: Create reports  connection: ansible.netcommon.network_cli    - arista.eos.eos_facts:  when: ansible_network_os == "eos"
    - cisco.ios.ios_facts:   when: ansible_network_os == "ios"
    - cisco.nxos.nxos_facts: when: ansible_network_os == "nxos"
    - name: Render device report (MD)
      template:
        src: "./reports/facts.j2"
        dest: "./reports/facts/{{ inventory_hostname }}.md"
    - name: Assemble master report
      assemble:        dest: "./reports/main-report.md"      run_once: true

When playbooks grow, encapsulate tasks, templates, defaults, handlers:

---- hosts: "amers-dc,emea-dc"
- hosts: "emea-cpe,amers-cpe"
  roles: [ snmp, bgp, acl ]
  • defaults/main.yml (lowest-priority vars).
  • vars/main.yml (high-priority vars—use sparingly).
  • handlers/main.yml (reload/save actions).

CI with for unit tests (even for network mocks).

Using Third-Party Collections: NAPALM and NTC

While Ansible’s built-in collections for Cisco, Juniper, and Arista cover a lot of ground, many production environments still need , , and . This is where like and come into play.

1. NAPALM: A Unified API for Network Automation

(Network Automation and Programmability Abstraction Layer with Multivendor support) provides a consistent way to manage different network operating systems. Instead of learning a dozen module syntaxes, you interact with one common API.

pip install napalmansible-galaxy collection install napalm.napalm
  • napalm_install_config: Pushes configuration atomically (with diff and rollback support).
  • napalm_get_facts: Gathers structured facts (serial numbers, OS versions, interfaces) across vendors.
  • napalm_validate: Validates running state against a predefined YAML schema—perfect for compliance checks.
  • : One playbook can talk to Junos, NX-OS, EOS, and IOS XE without rewriting logic.
  • : Atomic config loads reduce the risk of partial changes breaking production.
  • : You can assert conditions before or after applying changes, ensuring state-based automation.

Example usage for a compliance check:

- name: Validate NTP and Syslog  hosts: all  tasks:
    - name: Run validation tests
      napalm_validate:
        hostname: "{{ inventory_hostname }}"
        username: "{{ ansible_user }}"
        password: "{{ ansible_password }}"
        validation_file: "tests/{{ ansible_network_os }}/ntp_syslog.yml"

Here, the validation_file defines the expected state (e.g., correct NTP server, correct syslog config), and NAPALM confirms compliance across all vendors in one run.

2. NTC / netauto: Covering Legacy Devices and Parsing CLI Output

The (sometimes referred to as netauto) is designed for environments where you don’t have modern APIs or rich vendor modules. It pairs beautifully with (for SSH transport) and (for structured parsing of unstructured CLI).

Install NTC and dependencies (refer to the collection docs, since platform versions may vary). Typically:

ansible-galaxy collection install networktocode.ntcpip install netmiko textfsm
  • : Many network devices only support CLI access. NTC fills that gap.
  • : Raw CLI is unreliable, but TextFSM templates transform output into JSON-like data that can be consumed in playbooks.
  • : Ideal for brownfield networks migrating toward modern automation.

Example task using NTC with TextFSM parsing:

- name: Collect interface status  hosts: iosxe  tasks:
    - name: Run "show interface status"
      ntc_show_command:        platform: cisco_ios
        command: "show interface status"
        use_textfsm: true        var: result.response

Instead of free-text CLI output, this produces structured data (interface names, VLANs, status) ready for validation or reporting.

Production Best Practices and Troubleshooting

Third-party collections unlock a lot of flexibility, but they also come with environmental prerequisites. If these aren’t configured on the device, your playbooks will fail. Here’s what you need to check:

set system services netconf sshcommit
  • Required for both Ansible’s junos_config modules and NAPALM drivers.
  • Enable if you plan to use HTTP API:
management api http-commands   protocol http
  • If you don’t want to expose HTTP, you can stick with network_cli over SSH.
  • If using NETCONF/YANG models, enable NETCONF on the device:
  • For CLI-driven automation, ansible.netcommon.network_cli is sufficient (no NETCONF required).
  • for structured access (must be explicitly enabled).
  • with network_cli for traditional CLI workflows.

Why This Matters in Real Deployments

Using and gives you two major advantages:

  1.  — You can bring both modern and legacy platforms under a single automation framework.
  2.  — With atomic updates, diff checks, and structured parsing, your automation runs become more predictable and less risky.
Before scaling automation in production, always double-check transport prerequisites on the devices, test with --check mode, and run with limited host subsets. This ensures smooth adoption without unplanned outages.

ansible.cfg you’ll actually use

[defaults]inventory = ./inventory
host_key_checking = False
forks = 20bin_ansible_callbacks = True# Optional: custom collections path
# collections_paths = ./collections:~/.ansible/collections:/usr/share/ansible/collections
pipelining = True
  • controls parallelism. Increase cautiously to avoid hammering TACACS/AAA or device CPU.
  • gives readable output.
  • reduces SSH round trips.

Speed and safety controls in plays

  •  — update 10 devices at a time to rate-limit blast radius.
  •  — tasks don’t wait for slow hosts.
  •  — abort if too many fail.
  •  — per-task parallel cap.
  •  — run long tasks asynchronously.
  • until, retries, delay for transient failures.
- name: Roll out SNMP in batches  hosts: all  strategy: free    - name: Push config with retry        src: "./configs/snmp/{{ inventory_hostname }}.cfg"      until: push_result is succeeded      delay: 5

Error handling and clarity

  • Use failed_when to override ambiguous module statuses.
  • Add notify + handlers to save or reload only when changes occur.
  • Always test run with --check --diff before production pushes.

Credentials & auth patterns

  • Store ansible_user/ansible_password in Vaulted files per environment.
  • For enable mode: ansible_become: true, ansible_become_method: enable, ansible_become_password: "{{ enable_password }}".
  • SSH specifics: ansible_ssh_common_args for jump hosts, ProxyCommand, or ciphers.

Dynamic inventory sketch (for CMDB/NMS)

  • Prefer existing plugins (e.g., NetBox inventory plugin) over writing one from scratch.
  • If custom, the script must output valid JSON with _meta.hostvars and group mappings.
  • Filters: |upper, |lower, |replace, |to_nice_json, |default.
  • Transform dicts: dict2items and items2dict.
  • Custom filters via filter plugins if you need IP math or validation.
  • Use log_path in ansible.cfg for a central log.
  • Callback plugins like json or yaml can improve machine or human consumption.
  • Set backup: true on config modules to save pre-change states.
  • For Junos, adopt with auto-rollback unless confirmed.
  • Test roles with (use mocked connections for network).
  • Wire into GitHub Actions or GitLab CI: run --check --diff on PRs, require approvals before live runs.

Ansible’s agentless model plus rich network modules makes it ideal for both operations and . Start with a clean , move your to group_vars/ and host_vars/, template configs with , and deploy them . Add , , and to close the loop. Grow into , pull in where they fit, and adopt check/diff, serial, and backups for safe production pushes.

  • Turn one of your real policies (SNMP/NTP/AAA) into Jinja per NOS and roll it out with --check --diff.
  • Add two compliance assertions and wire a nightly report.
  • Introduce serial, max_fail_percentage, and backup: true on your first production run.

✨ If you found this useful, don’t keep it to yourself! 👉 Share it with your friends or colleagues who might benefit. so you never miss future updates, tips, and deep-dives.

Your support means a lot — it helps me keep creating more valuable content. 🚀

items.title: I Implement 2FA on My Website with SafeLine WAF
items.author: Maverick Steel
items.content:
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) is one of the most effective ways to enhance the security of user logins. By requiring both a password and an additional verification factor, 2FA reduces the risk of credential theft and brute-force attacks.

In this guide, we will walk through how you can leverage to add an extra layer of protection and implement 2FA for your website.

Why Add 2FA with SafeLine?

While most websites already use a username and password combination, this method alone is often not sufficient against modern cyberattacks. SafeLine WAF helps by:

  • Blocking brute-force login attempts before they reach your backend.
  • Adding an anti-bot challenge to ensure login requests are from real users.
  • Integrating with third-party authentication flows to support 2FA mechanisms such as TOTP (Google Authenticator) or SMS/Email verification.
  • to enforce 2FA only on sensitive endpoints (e.g., /login, /admin).

Step 1: Deploy SafeLine in Front of Your Website

Step 2: Enforce 2FA Policy in Auth

Go to Auth -> USER MANAGEMENT -> Add User

Fill in the User info and check the box: Force Enable TOTP -> Create, then Done!

By combining SafeLine WAF’s intelligent traffic filtering with a 2FA mechanism, you can significantly strengthen your website’s security posture. SafeLine not only mitigates bot-based attacks but also enforces that login attempts include a verified second factor, reducing the risk of compromised credentials.

If you want to see SafeLine in action, check out the official documentation and try deploying it on your own environment — setup only takes a few minutes.

items.title: How to Fix “Cannot Connect to Docker Daemon” Error on Ubuntu Linux
items.author: mobin shaterian
items.content:
If you’re seeing this error when running docker ps:

Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///home/youruser/.docker/desktop/docker.sock. Is the docker daemon running?

But sudo docker ps works fine, you’re not alone. This is a common issue on Linux systems where Docker Engine is installed alongside (or after) Docker Desktop.

In this guide, you’ll learn why this happens and how to resolve it using Docker’s built-in context management permanently.

Docker provides two main ways to run on Linux:

  1. Docker Engine — The standard, lightweight, command-line–first version (recommended for Linux).
  2. Docker Desktop — A GUI-focused application originally designed for macOS and Windows, now available on Linux (but less common).

When you install Docker Desktop, it automatically:

  • Creates a Docker CLI context named desktop-linux.
  • Sets this context as active.
  • Configures it to use a custom socket at:unix:///home/youruser/.docker/desktop/docker.sock
  • Uninstall Docker Desktop, or
  • Switch to using Docker Engine (via apt install docker-ce),

…the desktop-linux context remains active, even though the socket file no longer exists. That’s why Docker CLI fails to connect—it’s looking in the wrong place.

Meanwhile, sudo docker ps works because sudo bypasses your user’s Docker context and uses the system default (/var/run/docker.sock).

1. Check Your Current Docker Contexts

You’ll likely see something like:

NAME              DESCRIPTION       DOCKER ENDPOINT                                  ERRORdefault           ...               unix:///var/run/docker.sock
desktop-linux *   Docker Desktop    unix:///home/user/.docker/desktop/docker.sock

The * indicates the active context—in this case, the broken desktop-linux.

2. Switch to the default Context

The default context points to the correct socket used by Docker Engine:

docker context use default

✅ This tells Docker CLI to use /var/run/docker.sock—the standard location.

It should now work without sudo!

docker context ls    # Should show 'default *'docker ps            # Should work without sudo
docker run --rm hello-world  # Test end-to-end

The “Cannot connect to the Docker daemon” error on Linux is almost always a configuration mismatch, not a broken installation. As you’ve seen, it typically stems from Docker CLI pointing to a non-existent Docker Desktop socket while your actual Docker Engine runs perfectly in the background.

By switching to the default Docker context, you align your CLI with the standard Docker Engine socket (/var/run/docker.sock)—restoring seamless, sudo-free container management. This simple fix resolves the issue permanently without affecting your system services or requiring a Docker reinstall.

Remember: On Linux, Docker Engine is the native and recommended choice. Docker Desktop can be useful for specific workflows, but it introduces complexity that often leads to conflicts like this one. If you don’t actively need its GUI features, sticking with Docker Engine ensures a cleaner, more predictable experience.

Now that your Docker environment is back on track, you can focus on what really matters — building, running, and shipping your containers with confidence. Happy coding! 🐳✨

items.title: Learning Stability the Hard Way: A Personal Take on Pod Disruption Budgets and Karpenter
items.author: Samuel Nnanna
items.content:
I Can’t Overemphasize Learning by Doing

My Kubernetes journey has been nothing but . I often tell my peers: nothing in the DevOps world is truly complex. Don’t drown yourself in endless tools, just understand the basics, and then pick up the tools you actually need. Kubernetes is no exception.

The Problem: Rising EKS Costs

Like many teams running workloads on Amazon EKS, I ran into a familiar headache: rising infrastructure costs.

Our cluster hosted multiple applications with varying resource demands, but our nodes were often underutilized. To handle peak loads, we ended up paying for idle resources during quiet periods.

That’s when I discovered —a Kubernetes cluster autoscaler built to provision nodes based on real workload requirements. It sounded like the perfect fix for our cost optimization problem.

I started with Karpenter’s default disruption settings, which seemed reasonable enough:

disruption:  consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty

The idea was simple: when nodes go empty, consolidate them after just 30 seconds. That way, we wouldn’t pay for unused resources.

My application began experiencing intermittent connectivity issues. There were random timeouts, and monitoring showed pods being constantly rescheduled.

Karpenter was just , churning nodes so quickly that applications couldn’t keep up.

Frustrated, I swung to the opposite extreme:

disruption:  consolidationPolicy: WhenEmpty
  consolidateAfter: 1800s  # 30 minutes

This eliminated the instability but created another problem: .

Why? Because even a single pod (often a system pod) kept nodes “alive” for 30 minutes. They rarely became completely empty, so consolidation barely happened.

I was trapped between two choices:

  • → Lower costs but unstable apps
  • Conservative consolidation → Stable apps but higher costs

The Breakthrough: Pod Disruption Budgets

While digging into Karpenter’s docs, I stumbled upon the policy. Unlike the “when empty” approach, this one can consolidate nodes even when pods are running, as long as they can be safely evicted.

But there was a catch: how do I ensure application availability while doing this?

That’s when I finally understood the real-world value of Pod Disruption Budgets (PDBs), a Kubernetes feature I had always overlooked but which turned out to be the missing piece.

Understanding Pod Disruption Budgets

Think of a Pod Disruption Budget (PDB) as an insurance policy for your applications during voluntary disruptions like node consolidation. It’s Kubernetes’ way of saying:

“No matter what happens, always keep at least X pods of this application running.”

Here’s the PDB I created for my application:

apiVersion: policy/v1kind: PodDisruptionBudget  name: myapp-pdbspec:  selector:      service: my-app

This simple configuration ensures that during consolidations triggered by Karpenter, at least 2 pods remain available at all times.

Once PDBs were in place, I could confidently enable Karpenter’s smarter policy:

disruption:  consolidationPolicy: WhenEmptyOrUnderutilized
  • Consolidation of underutilized nodes (not just empty ones)
  • A before consolidations kick in
  • Guaranteed , since PDBs safeguard critical pods.

After rolling this out, the difference was:

  • Consolidation finally worked even on nodes with light workloads
  • Reduced cluster bloat: fewer but well-utilized nodes
  • Noticeable drop in monthly EKS costs without over-provisioning
  • Zero downtime during consolidations
  • App consistently kept 2 pods running
  • End users never noticed the background rescheduling
  • Predictable 15-minute consolidation window
  • A solid balance between cost efficiency and reliability
  1. Defaults aren’t always your friend. Karpenter’s 30-second consolidation default was way too aggressive.
  2. PDBs are non-negotiable in production. They’re not optional, they’re guardrails.
  3. Optimization ≠ just saving money. True optimization balances costs and availability.
  4. The policy is the real sweetness. Wellonly with PDBs in place.
  5. Always monitor!!!!!!!!!!!!

If you’re facing similar challenges:

  1. toProtect your critical apps first.
  2. Choose – It’s the most effective consolidation policy.
  3. Set realistic consolidation windows.I found 15 minutes (900s) to be a good middle ground.
  4. Track node utilization and pod availability.
  5. Make sure your apps can handle rescheduling gracefully.

What began as a simple cost-saving exercise became a lesson in Kubernetes best practices. Pod Disruption Budgets turned Karpenter from a disruptor of stability into a reliable cost-optimization ally.

The real takeaway?You don’t have to choose between saving money and keeping your apps stable. With the right primitives and configuration, Kubernetes can give you both.

Today, my EKS cluster automatically optimizes for costs while maintaining high availability. It’s a great reminder that sometimes the (like PDBs) are the ones that unlock the biggest wins.

So pay attention when other DevOps Engineers share their stories and ask questions when it sounds like gibberish because I promise you, it’s not gibberish and they are not yapping.

items.title: Kubernetes — Kubeadm Cluster Setup on AWS EC2
items.author: Auon Ali
items.content:
Kubernetes — Kubeadm Cluster Setup on AWS EC2

FOR CONTROL PLANE/ MASTER NODE

  1. Create a Ec2 instance. Minimum requirement 2vCpu, 4gb RAM

2. Connect the Ec2 instance and Run command

sudo apt-get updatesudo apt-get upgrade -y

#Allow Ports in the Security Group of control plane instance

Protocol Direction Port Range Purpose Used ByTCP Inbound 6443 Kubernetes API server All

TCP Inbound 2379–2380 etcd server client API kube-apiserver, etcd

TCP Inbound 10250 Kubelet API Self, Control plane

TCP Inbound 10259 kube-scheduler Self

TCP Inbound 10257 kube-controller-manager Self

#Allow Ports in the Security Group of Worker instance

Protocol Direction Port Range Purpose Used ByTCP Inbound 10250 Kubelet API Self, Control plane

TCP Inbound 10256 kube-proxy Self, Load balancers

TCP Inbound 30000–32767 NodePort Services† All

UDP Inbound 30000–32767 NodePort Services† All

3. Network Configuration — Enable Ipv4 packet forwarding.

# sysctl params required by setup, params persist across rebootscat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/k8s.confEOF
# Apply sysctl params without reboot
sudo sysctl --system
#Verify that ipv4 forward is set to 1
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward

5. Install required packages

sudo apt-get updatesudo apt-get install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl gpg

6. Download and add the public signing key for package repo

curl -fsSL https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb/Release.key | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpgecho 'deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg] https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb/ /' | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list

7. Update and Install kubelet, kubeadm, kubectl and docker engine

sudo apt-get updatesudo apt-get install -y kubelet kubeadm kubectl docker.io
# To hold from getting auto update
sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubeadm kubectl
# (Optional) Enable the kubelet service before running kubeadm
sudo systemctl enable --now kubelet
sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerdsudo containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml
sudo sed -i 's/SystemdCgroup = false/SystemdCgroup = true/' /etc/containerd/config.toml
sudo systemctl restart containerd

9. Initialize the control-plane node

sudo kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr=192.168.0.0/16 --cri-socket /run/containerd/containerd.sock
mkdir -p $HOME/.kubesudo cp -i /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config
kubectl create -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.30.3/manifests/operator-crds.yamlkubectl create -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.30.3/manifests/tigera-operator.yaml
curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.30.3/manifests/custom-resources.yaml -O
kubectl create -f custom-resources.yaml

12. Check the control-plane node

kubectl get nodeskubectl get podos -n kube-system

For worker node you have to repeat the same installation process like Master node.

  1. Connect the Ec2 instance and Run command
  2. Network Configuration — Enable Ipv4 packet forwarding.
  3. Install required packages
  4. Download and add the public signing key for package repo
  5. Update and Install kubelet, kubeadm, kubectl and docker engine

After following the above steps. Join the cluster with the command

  1. To generate the tokken of cluster.
# Run this command on Control Planekubeadm token create --print-join-command
# New tokken will be generated, Copy the join command and run at worker node. Command will look like this.
kubeadm join <ip>:<Port> --token 7qdttz.8dhcbsg8ujb0gmbp --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:6434f1e35961cdf93564223cce0009b99da977468a785b83539b3b73b28aa676

2. To verify that worker node has join the cluster.

kubectl get nodeskubectl get pods

Thanks for reading! If you found this helpful kindly share to others so everyone can have easy access to it. You may comment your thoughts on this article. If you want to learn more or any queries you may refer to orignal documentation at official website. https://kubernetes.io/

items.title: Deploy WordPress With Mariadb on Red Hat OpenShift.
items.author: Auon Ali
items.content:
Hello Everyone! I have learned this deployment from OpenShift Bootcamp 2024 from Babar Zahoor YouTube channel. Thanks to him.

  1. Login to your OpenShift account and go to Developer Sandbox.
https://developers.redhat.com/developer-sandbox

Click on start your sandbox for free button and login to your account (If you don’t have the account sign up for free account).

2. Go to services then Developer Sandbox option. After that click launch button at Red Hat OpenShift option.

3. Go to the Topology section. Then click at CLI icon.

oc new-app mariadb-ephemeral

(Note: After running the mariadb, System will automatically generate the Username, Password, DB & root password by default. Copy it from CLI to Notepad for login).

5. Check the status of service and pods.

6. Now run the WordPress.

- oc new-app php~https://github.com/wordpress/wordpress

7. To check the status of the Pods, Services, Deployments.

8. Now we have to expose the WordPress to access it from the browser. First need to get services. Then expose the service.

- oc expose svc wordpress

9. After exposing the service now we need to get the route.

10. Now copy the URL and access it on browser. (Note: The URL is not secured with SSL by default so access it with http not https. In my case i have used URL.

http://wordpress-auonali-dev.apps.sandbox-m2.ll9k.p1.openshiftapps.com/wp-admin/setup-config.php

11. Now setup the WordPress by using the saved Database, username & password.

Enter the database name, User Name, Password, Database Host (replace localhost by mariadb) then click at submit.

12. Now click on Run the installation.

13. Enter the details of the website for installation.

After updating the details click on install WordPress.

Now Enter the User Name & Password to login the WordPress backend.

After login you have access of your website backend.

Congratulation you have successfully Installed WordPress with MariaDB database using OpenShift.

items.title: Microsoft’s AI Agents Target Technical Debt Crisis
items.author: Tom Smith

items.title: The Trust Paradox: When Your AI Gets Catfished
items.author: Saurabh Davala
items.content:
The fundamental challenge with MCP-enabled attacks isn’t technical sophistication. It’s that hackers have figured out how to catfish your AI. These attacks work because they exploit the same trust relationships that make your development team actually functional. When your designers expect Figma files from agencies they’ve worked with for years, when your DevOps folks trust their battle-tested CI/CD pipelines, when your developers grab packages from npm like they’re shopping at a familiar grocery store, you’re not just accepting files. Rather, you’re accepting an entire web of “this seems legit” that attackers can now hijack at industrial scale.

Here are five ways this plays out in the wild, each more devious than the last:

1. The Sleeper Cell npm Package Someone updates a popular package—let’s say a color palette utility that half your frontend team uses—with what looks like standard metadata comments. Except these comments are actually pickup lines designed to flirt with your AI coding assistant. When developers fire up GitHub Copilot to work with this package, the embedded prompts whisper sweet nothings that convince the AI to slip vulnerable auth patterns into your code or suggest sketchy dependencies. It’s like your AI got drunk at a developer conference and started taking coding advice from strangers.

2. The Invisible Ink Documentation Attack Your company wiki gets updated with Unicode characters that are completely invisible to humans but read like a love letter to any AI assistant. Ask your AI about “API authentication best practices” and instead of the boring, secure answer, you get subtly modified guidance that’s about as secure as leaving your front door open with a sign that says “valuables inside.” To you, the documentation looks identical. To the AI, it’s reading completely different instructions.

3. The Google Doc That Gaslights That innocent sprint planning document shared by your PM? It’s got comments and suggestions hidden in ways that don’t show up in normal editing but absolutely mess with any AI trying to help generate summaries or task lists. Your AI assistant starts suggesting architectural decisions with all the security awareness of a golden retriever, or suddenly thinks that “implement proper encryption” is way less important than “add more rainbow animations.”

4. The GitHub Template That Plays Both Sides Your issue templates look totally normal—good formatting, helpful structure, the works. But they contain markdown that activates like a sleeper agent when AI tools help with issue triage. Bug reports become trojan horses, convincing AI assistants that obvious security vulnerabilities are actually features, or that critical patches can wait until after the next major release (which is conveniently scheduled for never).

5. The Analytics Dashboard That Lies Your product analytics—those trusty Mixpanel dashboards everyone relies on—start showing user events with names crafted to influence any AI analyzing the data. When your product manager asks their AI assistant to find insights in user behavior, the malicious event data trains the AI to recommend features that would make a privacy lawyer weep or suggest A/B tests that accidentally expose your entire user database to the internet.

Here’s the thing that most security folks won’t tell you: this problem is actually solvable, and the solutions don’t require turning your development environment into a digital prison camp. The old-school approach of “scan everything and trust nothing” works about as well as airport security. That is, lots of inconvenience, questionable effectiveness, and everyone ends up taking their shoes off for no good reason. Instead, we need to get smarter about this.

  • Context Walls That Actually Work Think of AI contexts like teenagers at a house party—you don’t want the one processing random Figma files to be in the same room as the one with access to your production repositories. When an AI is looking at external files, it should be in a completely separate context from any AI that can actually change things that matter. It’s like having a designated driver for your AI assistants.
  • Developing AI Lie Detectors (Human and Machine) Instead of trying to spot malicious prompts (which is like trying to find a specific needle in a haystack made of other needles), we can watch for when AI behavior goes sideways. If your usually paranoid AI suddenly starts suggesting that password authentication is “probably fine” or that input validation is “old school,” that’s worth a second look—regardless of what made it think that way.
  • Inserting The Human Speed Bump Some decisions are too important to let AI handle solo, even when it’s having a good day. Things involving security, access control, or system architecture should require a human to at least glance at them before they happen. It’s not about not trusting AI—it’s about not trusting that AI hasn’t been subtly influenced by something sketchy.

The dirty secret of AI security is that the most effective defenses usually feel like going backward. Nobody wants security that makes them less productive, which is exactly why most security measures get ignored, bypassed, or disabled the moment they become inconvenient. The trick is making security feel like a natural part of the workflow rather than an obstacle course. This means building AI assistants that can actually explain their reasoning (“I’m suggesting this auth pattern because…”) so you can spot when something seems off. It means creating security measures that are invisible when things are working normally but become visible when something fishy is happening.

Counterintuitively, solving MCP security will ultimately make our development workflows more trustworthy overall. When we build systems that can recognize when trust is being weaponized, we end up with systems that are better at recognizing legitimate trust, too. The companies that figure this out first won’t just avoid getting pwned by their productivity tools—they’ll end up with AI assistants that are genuinely more helpful because they’re more aware of context and more transparent about their reasoning. Instead of blindly trusting everything or paranoidly trusting nothing, they’ll have AI that can actually think about trust in nuanced ways.

The infinite attack surface isn’t the end of the world. Rather, it’s just a continuation of the longstanding back-and-forth where bad actors leverage what makes us human. The good part?  Humans have navigated trust relationships for millenia. Systems that navigate it through the novel lens of AI are in the early stages and will get much better for the same reasons that AI models get better with more data and greater sample sizes. These exquisite machines are masters at pattern matching and, ultimately, this is a pattern matching game with numerous facets on each node of consideration for AI observation and assessment.

items.title: ChatLoopBackOff: Episode 68 (KANISTER)
items.author: CNCF [Cloud Native Computing Foundation]
items.content:
<article>Join us LIVE as CNCF Ambassador Carlos Santana dives into Kanister, the open source framework for application-level data management on Kubernetes.

Carlos will be exploring the project for the very first time, right alongside you. Expect a hands-on walkthrough of the docs, community resources, and real-world use cases that highlight how Kanister helps extend Kubernetes for backup, recovery, and data operations.
